item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The alien slug monster is funny, well written, AND agrees with me about Kant? I couldn't +1 hard enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback! I had so much fun writing Sluggy’s dialogue. As for the first interview, my intention was to show that the interviewer was thrown off by the idea that this creature knew what a nickname was. Like, “is it asking if I have a nickname? Or is this some alien thing that I’m not getting?’ But I can see how that might not come across well.

Regardless, I’m glad you liked it in the end!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Almost no vote because this is close to 1171, which I loved but that doesn't mean I want clones of it. Also the first interview didn't do it for me. I get maybe the doctor had not interviewed an inhuman being before, but they didn't seem to understand the concept of a nickname?

But then the next interviews came, and oh boy Sluggy made it for me. He was the right mix of alien but not weird-for-weirdness sake. The snapshot of how stressful working for the Foundation can be (when its not actively life threatening) was simply icing on the cake.

item.title: very nice
item.content:encoded:
Normally I'm not that huge a fan of dialogue based SCP's, but this one really knocked it out of the ballpark. Great job!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 :)
This is Amazing! I honestly think this is the best dialogue I have read in an SCP but I do have a suggestion I think this needs more of a hook to it. It starts off kinda like your run of the mill SCP which doesn't reflect the quality of the dialogue. While I already think this is amazing I think a slight improved hook could have hooked in a few more reviewers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The slug just repeatedly trolling the JR made my day. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know. The characters and dialogue just fall flat for me and the whole premise makes me confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We stan Sluggy in this household. +1.

also its in boston so you get an invisible cool point

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback! Early drafts of this piece had even longer descriptions, and I took a ruthless slash and burn approach to cutting down the unnecessary fluff. In retrospect, you’re right, I could probably have been even more thorough!

I couldn’t really bring myself to ditch some of the more cliche ideas, though that also might have improved the piece. As a compromise to myself, I had a bit of a fun time coming up with a silly-sounding “3 x 3 x 1 x 1 meter 4-doorway” as a bit of a dig at the sometimes overzealous con procs that show up in articles. I considered trying to come up with an in-story reason why it would have to be made of titanium, but I thought that would be going a bit too far. :p

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded:
I was a bit sketchy about the set up but as soon as the interview logs hit I knew I found a new favorite.

+1 for making me laugh during class. The teacher gave me funny looks until the end of it.

The whole monster love theme went over my head, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this might be some of the best dialogue on the entire site.

an enthusiastic +1. i can see why this made the front page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking hilarious and I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're creeping close to 100, and this deserves that milestone. Hilarious, yet in a way where I felt like it was entirely in character and plausible in-world. I love that Sluggy is a extradimensional scientist who is fucking around with the humans from jump.

Great all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Several instances of SCP-4357-0 were encountered in the process, all of whom displayed some degree of non-violent, non-aggressive hostility to the task force.

This entire article is gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is great! I have a real weak spot for casual alien scps, like 1171. it's now my headcanon that sluggy and beauremont are the same species. maybe not from the same dimension though, considering the mammal comment…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You almost lost me with the technocratic description and using a dimension door to have yet another alternate dimension where the world is inhabited by kooky creatures. But the interviews make this relatable to a general audience, reminds me of 1171 in a good way.

There’s personality oozing out of the logs here which makes up for a somewhat boring description relying on cliches. I might try to move some of that information to the con procs to make the description more of a transition to the interviews but that’s just me. This works very well as-is. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is flat out wonderful and I loved every minute of it. If I had one complaint to make, it's that the setup dragged just a hair, making me wish it got to the good stuff just a mite faster. Hardly a critique though, given the comfortably brisk pace at which the piece flows.

This was very good and I hope it slowly claws its way up in votes. +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“A standard steel-reinforced 3 x 3 x 1 x 1 meter 4-doorway is to be hyperspatially installed around SCP-4357”

I'm trying to indicate that this is a 4-dimensional portal, isolating it from parallel realities as explained later.

Use meters instead of feet.

If you're referring to the American 6'8" doorway, that's a standardized size for doorframes in the American construction industry. Saying a "2.032 meter door" wouldn't make sense, when the common term is "6-8 door." The Foundation constructs and measures everything in metric, but most of America doesn't, so using standard industry terms sometimes means using Imperial.

Another example of this would be if the Foundation comes across something that had been built with 1/4 inch screws. It wouldn't make sense to say "6.35 millimeter screws," because the item that is called a "1/4 inch screw" refers to a specific, standardized product with a particular thread count. There's no such thing as a "6.35 millimeter screw."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great story +1, the interviews were fun and easy to read. Just 2 things bothered me:

“A standard steel-reinforced 3 x 3 x 1 x 1 meter 4-doorway is to be hyperspatially installed around SCP-4357” I didn’t understand any of this, please write easier.

Use meters instead of feet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like people would be happier if they would just stop repressing their latent monsterfucker tendencies and just be honest with themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can just say all readers are cowards and can't appreciate love between a human and a horrifying worm creature. It's okay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I’m a bit sad about this piece’s performance as well. They can’t all be winners, of course, but I’m more proud of this article than some of my much higher-rated pieces. I wrote it largely as an exercise in storytelling through dialogue, and regardless of the score, I’m happy to see that it worked!

I think part of why it didn’t take off is that it’s much lighter on horror than many scp’s. It’s always important to know your audience when writing, and it looks like I missed the mark there.

If you liked it, I’d suggest checking out SCP-4170. It’s much less dialogue heavy, but I wrote it as a character study.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How have I never commented on this?

From the first read through to the tenth, this is one of my favorite articles on the site. It so perfectly blends Foundationverse lore/policy, actual interesting anomaly, and OUTSTANDING dialogue and characters. The fact that this has stagnated at +38 is shocking to me, and I hope that more people get the chance to read this in the future, because it is an absolute pleasure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Following preliminary establishment of friendly relations

lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It really is the way that the aliens have maxxed out their stats in sarcasm that makes this article delightful to read.

Also I like the social commentary on the consequences of having to study an anomaly in sito.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4357-0-A: I DON'T THINK THAT'S RIGHT. YOU LOOK LIKE A DICK TO ME.

Dr. Giordano: I'm not sure what you mean.

Can't stop laughing at this! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-4357-0-A: I DON'T KNOW, I'M JUST A SLUG. HOW MUCH LONGER WILL HE LIVE?
~
Dr. Harlan: Well, he's about fifty, so maybe another forty years?
~
SCP-4357-0-A: HE CAN HAVE THEM IN FORTY-ONE.

Three cheers for the sassy slug monster. +1

Also I'm adding to my headcanon that most anomaly interviewers tend to be sarcasm-blind, which all the skips have fun with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is canon now.

(I was trying to go for "Dr. Harlan thought Sluggy was his friend," but hey, death of the author and all that. Your reading actually does make more sense than I realized).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This danced up to the border between main list and-J, then danced beautifully up and down along it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun fact: After my initial read of the last interview, I came away convinced that Harlan had realized he didn't love his wife and was trying to romantically pursue Sluggy through the interview process. I must have missed some lines somewhere.

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to RockTeethMothEyes and Pedantique for their critique and advice!

Dr. Harlan quit the Foundation, patched things together with his wife, and now they live together in upstate New York, where he teaches high school biology and has never been happier. Sluggy went on to author several prestigious papers in his universe, and wrote a bestselling book about his experiences with humans.

If you liked this story, check out some of my other work on my Author Page!

EDIT: As per Pedantique's thoughts below, Dr. Harlan is in love with Sluggy and this is a tragic tale of star-crossed lovers.
